title: How U.S. Forces Save Downed Air Crews
summary: This article explains how U.S. military teams work to find and rescue pilots whose planes have crashed in combat. It features an interview with retired Master Sergeant Wes Bryant, who has experience in Air Force special operations. He describes that search and rescue missions have two main parts. First, they use all available resources like planes, intelligence, and local helpers to locate the missing crew member. Second, they protect the rescue planes from enemy air defenses, as Iran still has strong defenses despite claims of U.S. air superiority. The goal is to safely bring the crew back home.

background_read: Search and rescue missions are important operations where military teams work to save people in dangerous situations, like after a plane crash. In combat, pilots might get shot down, and rescuers use planes, spies, and local friends to find them while protecting themselves from enemy attacks. This helps keep aircrew safe and is a key part of military teamwork.
Article_Structure: WHO: Retired Master Sergeant Wes Bryant and U.S. forces
WHAT: Conduct search and rescue for downed combat air crews
WHERE: In places like Iran where missions might occur
WHY: To locate and safely rescue missing crew members and protect assets from air defenses

perspectives.description: Wes Bryant explains the practical steps and challenges in search and rescue missions based on his experience.
